frameworks.name: visual working memory distortions
frameworks.one_line: item representations in VWM can attract toward or repel from each other

frameworks.name: ensemble / summary statistics encoding
frameworks.one_line: attraction biases exploit group-level information to stabilize memory

frameworks.name: discriminability / repulsion account
frameworks.one_line: repulsion reflects a tradeoff for distinct representations and grows with delay

core_question: Under what conditions does visual working memory produce attractive versus repulsive distortions between item representations?
key_assumptions: memory capacity constrains accuracy when many items must be held
attractive and repulsive biases reflect genuine memory representation changes, not response bias
memory representations can evolve dynamically during the delay
manipulations: set size (many items vs. few items)
similarity of remembered items (highly similar but discernible)
tasks designed to mitigate response bias
key_findings: high set sizes (near capacity) yield attraction between item memories
small sets of similar items yield repulsion between item memories
repulsion strengthens with longer memory delays
both biases persist under response-bias-controlled designs
interpretations: attraction stabilizes memory by leveraging ensemble information across items
repulsion reflects a tradeoff favoring distinct, discriminable representations
